content.full_text:
R. CLIFFORD LOBER
Attorney at Law
(707) 224-7444

February 13, 1990

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Commissioners:

I represent a non-profit corporation which has been formed to manage
certain lands belonging to Napa Valley Community College District (hereinafter
"College"). The Corporation (hereinafter "NAVCO") is not a foundation formed
under Education Code Section 72670 regarding the establishment of auxiliary
organizations.

A change in the Board of Directors of NAVCO is contemplated which could
require NAVCO to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. Set forth below is the
present and proposed composition of NAVCO. I request an informal opinion on
the need for a Conflict of Interest Code; i.e., is NAVCO an "agency" under
the Act?

FACTS

The College has real property not needed for classrooms. To generate
income the College asked the administration for ideas on the use of the land.
The administration determined a lease, not a sale would be best. A third party
suggested that a non profit corporation as lessee would be more efficient than a
College lease directly to a developer. The non-profit (NAVCO) would sublease
to the developer. No sublease has yet been made.

NAVCO was formed with 5 directors and no stockholders with the
knowledge of the College. A Superior Court judge independently appointed
members of the business community as the 5 initial directors. The by-laws
provide that any vacancies are filled by the remaining directors. No College
employee or official is on the board.

NAVCO then offered to lease the subject land, some 45 acres, pursuant to
Education Code procedures. Required notices were published in newspapers,
public hearings were held and the competitive bidding process was followed.
The College awarded the lease to NAVCO. The college lease to NAVCO is for
99 years. NAVCO would sublease to a developer for a lesser term.

The Corporation has been granted a 501(c)(3) tax exempt status by the
Internal Revenue Service. The Directors receive no compensation of any type
and there are no public monies involved in that all expenses of NAVCO for
management of the land are to be paid by the developers of the land. All
income to NAVCO above necessary expenses is remitted to the college in the
form of rent and gifts. The activities of NAVCO are limited by the tax
exemption to higher education in general and Napa Valley Community College
specifically.

FIVE FINANCIAL PLAZA • SUITE 120 • NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94558

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 13, 1990
Page Two


     The only legal ties between the college and NAVCO consist of the leases
and contracts which specify the type of use NAVCO may obtain for the land,
caps on expenses, accounting requirements and the right to review plans for
structures on the property. The college has no control except this contractual
control as a landlord and all transactions are at arms length. The college
agrees to grant reasonable easements and cooperate with local government
authorities in matters such as rezoning.

     The college is considering assuming more than contractural control by
requesting an increase in the number of board members from five to seven,
with the two additional members appointed by the college.

                              DISCUSSION

     I have your November 17, 1986 opinion designated File #A-86-214 regarding
the West Valley Community College District Foundation. In it you set forth
certain criteria. Applying the criteria I have the following observations.

     The first criterion is whether the impetus for formation of the entity was
with a governmental body. The answer should be no. NAVCO was formed at
the suggestion of a third party and the Board consented to the idea. NAVCO
obtained a lease by noticed competitive bidding under the code.

     The second criterion is whether all or most of the entities funds are
received from public sources. The District has leased to NAVCO 45 acres of
land for $1158.00 per acre per year. The college felt this was a fair market
value for unimproved land in Napa, one half of which was unusable as wet
lands, and which required a great deal of off-site improvement order to
accommodate a development. The proposed development is a hotel and,
depending on the class of hotel obtained, and the percentage rent NAVCO could
obtain, the income could be quite high. The present developer interested in
the property for a hotel would guarantee one million dollars per year income
after the fourth operating year of the hotel. Prior to that, the developer has
agreed to reimburse NAVCO for the rental amounts owed to the college and pay
NAVCO operating expenses so that no public money is involved. All of the one
million dollars, with the exception of three or four percent for audits, legal
costs and reserves, would go to the college as rent (45 x 1158 = 52110) and the
balance as a gift. The rent is not a token amount.

     The third criterion is whether the entity is performing a function which
public agencies are legally authorized to perform. And the answer is yes, the
district could do this directly.

     The last criterion is whether the entity is treated as public by other
statutory provisions. The appointment by the college of two members could
constitute this a committee created by the College, and under Gov. Code
54952.2 and 54952.3. NAVCO could be subject to the Brown Act (open meeting

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 13, 1990
Page Three


law) and the Public Agenda requirements of the Government Code. This would
require all meetings to be noticed and held in public. NAVCO would not be
subject to the State Contracts Code, the Davis-Bacon Act, and other statutory
provisions since all construction would be by a private entity and the
structures would not be owned or used by the college during the term of the
lease. At the end of 99 years all structures belong to the College.

None of the original five members of NAVCO receive any compensation but
the two new members would be college officials or employees and paid as such.
My request for an opinion is as to whether or not NAVCO would be required to
adopt a Conflict of Interest Code if the college appoints two board members.

Very truly yours,


R. Clifford Lober

RCL:cn

sections.question_synthetic: Does NAVCO, a non-profit corporation leasing college land, qualify as an 'agency' under the Political Reform Act requiring adoption of a Conflict of Interest Code if the college appoints two additional board members?
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney, not an FPPC response. The attorney references a prior FPPC opinion (File #A-86-214) and applies its criteria to the current fact pattern but provides no FPPC conclusion. Document contains facts and discussion but no formal Conclusion or Answer section from FPPC. High confidence in identification as incoming request; moderate confidence in synthetic question given mixed formatting.

embedding.summary: An attorney requests an informal FPPC opinion on whether NAVCO, a non-profit corporation formed to manage Napa Valley Community College District land through a lease arrangement, would be required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code if the college appoints two additional board members.
